content.text: Administrative Units (AUs) are containers in Microsoft Entra ID that limit the scope of role assignments. Without AUs, a User Administrator role grants permissions over every user in the entire tenant. With an AU, that same role can be constrained to manage only the users, groups, and devices within a specific AU — enabling regional IT teams, department admins, or helpdesk staff to operate without over-privileged tenant-wide access.

AUs do not change authentication, authorization, or Conditional Access behaviour — they are purely a delegation boundary. A user can belong to multiple AUs simultaneously, and AU membership has no effect on the user's own resource access.
AUs support two membership types: Assigned and Dynamic.
Manually by an admin (portal or Graph API)
Automatically via a membership rule (same syntax as dynamic groups)
Attribute-driven — e.g. all corporate devices, all Sales department users
Only AU-scoped admins can add/remove members
Sensitive populations where membership itself is controlled

Restricted AUs add a layer of membership governance: even Global Administrators cannot directly add members — only administrators scoped to that AU can. This is useful for regulated employee populations.
AUs are created through the Entra ID portal (Identity > Administrative Units > New) or via Microsoft Graph. The following example creates an assigned AU and populates it with users and groups.

# Create an Administrative Unit and add members via Microsoft Graph
import requests

TENANT_ID = "YOUR_TENANT_ID"
CLIENT_ID = "YOUR_APP_ID"
CLIENT_SECRET = "YOUR_SECRET"

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token",
        data={
            "grant_type": "client_credentials",
            "client_id": CLIENT_ID,
            "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET,
            "scope": "https://graph.microsoft.com/.default",
        }
    )
    return r.json()["access_token"]

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# 1. Create the Administrative Unit
au_payload = {
    "displayName": "West Region",
    "description": "Users and devices in the West region",
    "membershipType": "Assigned",    # or "Dynamic" for dynamic AUs
    # For restricted AU: all members can only be managed through this AU
    "membershipRule": None,
    "membershipRuleProcessingState": None,
}
resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits",
    headers=headers,
    json=au_payload
)
au = resp.json()
au_id = au["id"]
print(f"Created AU: {au['displayName']} ({au_id})")

# 2. Add a user to the AU
user_object_id = "aaaaaaaa-bbbb-cccc-dddd-eeeeeeeeeeee"
requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits/{au_id}/members/$ref",
    headers=headers,
    json={"@odata.id": f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{user_object_id}"}
)

# 3. Add a group to the AU
group_object_id = "ffffffff-gggg-hhhh-iiii-jjjjjjjjjjjj"
requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits/{au_id}/members/$ref",
    headers=headers,
    json={"@odata.id": f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups/{group_object_id}"}
)
print("Members added.")

The power of AUs comes from scoped role assignments: assigning a directory role to a principal but restricting that role's effect to the members of a specific AU. Supported roles include User Administrator, Helpdesk Administrator, Groups Administrator, Password Administrator, and Cloud Device Administrator.

Not all Entra ID roles support AU scoping — only a defined subset of roles that operate on users, groups, or devices can be scoped. Roles like Global Administrator, Conditional Access Administrator, and Security Administrator are always tenant-wide.

# Assign a directory role scoped to an Administrative Unit
# This is the core delegation model: admins only see/manage AU members

import requests

def assign_scoped_role(token, au_id, user_id, role_template_id):
    """
    role_template_id examples:
      User Administrator:     fe930be7-5e62-47db-91af-98c3a49a38b1
      Helpdesk Administrator: 729827e3-9c14-49f7-bb1b-9608f156bbb8
      Groups Administrator:   fdd7a751-b60b-444a-984c-02652fe8fa1c
      Cloud Device Admin:     7698a772-787b-4ac8-901f-60d6b08affd2
    """
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}
    
    # Step 1: look up the roleDefinitionId from the template
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directoryRoles?$filter=roleTemplateId eq '{role_template_id}'",
        headers=headers
    )
    roles = resp.json().get("value", [])
    if not roles:
        # Activate the role in the tenant first
        act = requests.post(
            "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directoryRoles",
            headers=headers,
            json={"roleTemplateId": role_template_id}
        )
        role_id = act.json()["id"]
    else:
        role_id = roles[0]["id"]
    
    # Step 2: create the scoped member assignment
    assignment = {
        "@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.scopedRoleMembership",
        "roleId": role_id,
        "administrativeUnitId": au_id,
        "roleMemberInfo": {
            "@odata.type": "#microsoft.graph.identity",
            "id": user_id
        }
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits/{au_id}/scopedRoleMembers",
        headers=headers,
        json=assignment
    )
    print(f"Scoped role assigned: {resp.status_code} {resp.json().get('id')}")

token = get_token()
assign_scoped_role(
    token,
    au_id="<au-object-id>",
    user_id="<helpdesk-admin-object-id>",
    role_template_id="729827e3-9c14-49f7-bb1b-9608f156bbb8"  # Helpdesk Admin
)
Create/update/delete users in AU, assign licenses, reset passwords
Delete admin accounts, change roles, manage non-AU users
Reset passwords for non-admin AU members
Reset passwords for admins or users outside the AU
Manage groups outside the AU or add users from outside
Reset passwords for non-privileged users in AU
Reset passwords for admins or non-AU users

A dynamic AU maintains its membership automatically based on a rule evaluated against directory object attributes. The rule syntax is identical to dynamic group membership rules. Dynamic AUs require Entra ID P1 licensing.

# Create a dynamic Administrative Unit for devices
# Dynamic AUs automatically populate via a membership rule
# Requires: Entra ID P1 or higher

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# Dynamic AU for Corporate-owned Windows devices
dynamic_au_payload = {
    "displayName": "Corporate Devices",
    "description": "All Intune-enrolled, corporate-owned Windows devices",
    "membershipType": "Dynamic",
    "membershipRule": "(device.deviceOwnership -eq \"Company\") and (device.operatingSystem -eq \"Windows\")",
    "membershipRuleProcessingState": "On",   # enables the rule
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits",
    headers=headers,
    json=dynamic_au_payload
)
au = resp.json()
print(f"Dynamic AU created: {au['displayName']} ({au['id']})")
print(f"Rule: {au.get('membershipRule')}")
print(f"Processing state: {au.get('membershipRuleProcessingState')}")

# Rule syntax is the same as dynamic group rules
# Common device rule attributes:
#   device.deviceOwnership         -eq "Company" / "Personal"
#   device.operatingSystem         -eq "Windows" / "iOS" / "Android"
#   device.managementType          -eq "MDM"
#   device.trustType               -eq "AzureAD" / "Workplace" / "ServerAD"
#   device.deviceCategory          -eq "BYOD"
#   device.enrollmentProfileName   -startsWith "Autopilot-"
#   device.extensionAttribute1     -eq "Sales"

For initial population or periodic synchronisation of large AUs, the Microsoft Graph PowerShell SDK is the most practical tool. The following script reads a CSV and adds users to an AU with error handling.
Authentication Method Policies Scoped to AUs

Authentication method policies (which MFA methods users can register and use) can be targeted at AUs rather than the whole tenant. This enables phased rollouts — enabling passkeys or Microsoft Authenticator for a pilot AU before expanding tenant-wide.

# Apply an authentication method policy scoped to an AU
# (Requires: AuthenticationPolicy.ReadWrite.All, Policy.ReadWrite.AuthenticationMethod)
# Example: enable Microsoft Authenticator only for AU members

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# Get current Authenticator policy
resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationMethodsPolicy/authenticationMethodConfigurations/MicrosoftAuthenticator",
    headers=headers
)
policy = resp.json()
print("Current state:", policy.get("state"))

# Include a specific AU in the Authenticator policy (in addition to any existing includes)
# This lets you enable Authenticator for West Region users without tenant-wide rollout
au_id = "<west-region-au-id>"

include_target = {
    "targetType": "administrativeUnit",
    "id": au_id
}

existing_includes = policy.get("includeTargets", [])
existing_includes.append(include_target)

patch_body = {
    "state": "enabled",
    "includeTargets": existing_includes
}

resp = requests.patch(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationMethodsPolicy/authenticationMethodConfigurations/MicrosoftAuthenticator",
    headers=headers,
    json=patch_body
)
print(f"Updated Authenticator policy: {resp.status_code}")

Regular audits of your AU structure should verify that AUs cover the expected population, scoped role assignments are accurate, and no unexpectedly privileged accounts exist. The following script enumerates all AUs, their member counts, and scoped role members.

# Audit all AUs in the tenant and their scoped role assignments
import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

# List all AUs
aus_resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits"
    "?$select=id,displayName,membershipType,membershipRuleProcessingState",
    headers=headers
)
aus = aus_resp.json().get("value", [])
print(f"Total AUs: {len(aus)}\n")

for au in aus:
    au_id = au["id"]
    mtype = au.get("membershipType", "Assigned")
    print(f"AU: {au['displayName']} ({au_id}) [{mtype}]")
    
    # List member count
    members_resp = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits/{au_id}/members/$count",
        headers={**headers, "ConsistencyLevel": "eventual"}
    )
    if members_resp.ok:
        print(f"  Members: {members_resp.text}")
    
    # List scoped role members
    roles_resp = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/administrativeUnits/{au_id}/scopedRoleMembers",
        headers=headers
    )
    for sr in roles_resp.json().get("value", []):
        print(f"  Scoped admin: {sr['roleMemberInfo'].get('displayName','?')} -> role {sr['roleId'][:8]}...")
    print()
AU scoped admin resets a tenant admin's password
Helpdesk Admin cannot reset passwords of Directory Readers or above — correct by design
AU shows 0 members despite matching users
Check membershipRuleProcessingState is 'On'; allow up to 24h for initial sync
Global Admin can temporarily remove the restriction flag, add members, then re-enable
Portal doesn't show the AU as scope option
Only ~10 roles support AU scoping; use tenant-wide assignment for unsupported roles
Scoped admin A can reset user's password unexpectedly
Membership in one AU doesn't grant another AU's admin access; review all AU memberships
SSPR and Authentication Policies Scoped to AUs

Some Entra ID policies can be targeted at AUs rather than the full tenant, enabling a phased rollout approach. Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) is typically targeted at security groups; for AU-aligned rollouts, create a dynamic group mirroring the AU membership rule and target SSPR at that group.

Microsoft Authenticator and other authentication method policies in the Authentication Methods Policy support direct AU targeting via the Graph API, making AUs the preferred scope unit for new MFA method rollouts.

# Configure SSPR (Self-Service Password Reset) to be scoped to an AU
# This allows you to enable SSPR for a pilot group before tenant-wide rollout
# Requires: Policy.ReadWrite.AuthenticationMethod, AuthenticationPolicy.ReadWrite.All

import requests

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# Get current SSPR policy
resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationMethodsPolicy",
    headers=headers
)
policy = resp.json()
print("Registration campaign state:", policy.get("registrationEnforcement", {}).get("authenticationMethodsRegistrationCampaign", {}).get("state"))

# Target SSPR enablement to a specific AU
# Note: SSPR enablement itself is via password reset policy, not auth methods policy
# Patch the SSPR policy to target users in an AU via the legacy SSPR API

# Modern approach via Entra admin center or PowerShell
# (The Graph API for SSPR targeting is via the passwordResetPolicies resource)
sspr_config_response = requests.patch(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/passwordResetPolicies",
    headers=headers,
    json={
        "selfServicePasswordResetEnabled": True,
        # "selected" means only specific groups/AUs; "all" means everyone
        "enabledForScope": "selected",
        # Cannot directly target AUs in SSPR policy via beta API — use a group
        # that mirrors AU membership via dynamic group rule instead
    }
)
print(f"SSPR patch: {sspr_config_response.status_code}")

# PRACTICAL ALTERNATIVE: Mirror AU dynamic membership rule in a Security Group
# Create a dynamic group with the same rule, then assign SSPR to that group
group_payload = {
    "displayName": "SSPR-Pilot-WestRegion",
    "groupTypes": ["DynamicMembership"],
    "membershipRule": "(user.department -eq \"West Region\") and (user.accountEnabled -eq true)",
    "membershipRuleProcessingState": "On",
    "securityEnabled": True,
    "mailEnabled": False,
    "mailNickname": "SSPR-Pilot-WestRegion"
}
group_resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups",
    headers=headers,
    json=group_payload
)
print(f"Mirror group: {group_resp.status_code} {group_resp.json().get('id')}")
Managing Administrative Units as Code with Terraform

For organisations managing Entra ID through infrastructure-as-code, the AzureAD Terraform provider exposes resources for creating AUs, managing their membership, and assigning scoped roles. This enables AU structure to be version-controlled, reviewed, and applied through CI/CD pipelines.
Always store the AU structure and scoped role assignments in Terraform (or Bicep) alongside the identity objects themselves. AU structure is infrastructure — treat it as code.

Sensitive AUs (executive populations, privileged access groups, compliance-regulated users) should be monitored for unexpected membership changes. Microsoft Graph change notifications (webhooks) can alert your SIEM or incident response pipeline within seconds of a membership modification.

# Monitor AU membership changes via Microsoft Graph change notifications
# Useful for detecting unauthorized additions or removals from sensitive AUs

import json, hmac, hashlib, requests
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify

app = Flask(__name__)
TENANT_ID = "YOUR_TENANT_ID"
AU_ID = "YOUR_SENSITIVE_AU_ID"
NOTIFICATION_SECRET = "YOUR_RANDOM_SECRET_64CHARS"

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token",
        data={
            "grant_type": "client_credentials",
            "client_id": "YOUR_APP_ID",
            "client_secret": "YOUR_APP_SECRET",
            "scope": "https://graph.microsoft.com/.default",
        }
    )
    return r.json()["access_token"]

def subscribe_to_au_changes(token):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}
    sub = {
        "changeType": "updated",
        "notificationUrl": "https://your-webhook.example.com/au-changes",
        "resource": f"/directory/administrativeUnits/{AU_ID}/members",
        "expirationDateTime": "2026-08-01T00:00:00Z",
        "clientState": NOTIFICATION_SECRET,
        "includeResourceData": False,   # True requires encryption setup
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/subscriptions",
        headers=headers,
        json=sub
    )
    print(f"Subscription: {resp.status_code}", resp.json().get("id"))

@app.route("/au-changes", methods=["POST"])
def handle_notification():
    # Microsoft sends a validation token on subscription creation
    val_token = request.args.get("validationToken")
    if val_token:
        return val_token, 200, {"Content-Type": "text/plain"}
    
    data = request.get_json()
    for n in data.get("value", []):
        # Verify the client state
        if n.get("clientState") != NOTIFICATION_SECRET:
            continue
        resource = n.get("resource", "")
        print(f"AU membership changed: {resource} at {n.get('subscriptionExpirationDateTime')}")
        # Trigger your alerting logic here (SIEM, email, Teams message)
    
    return jsonify({}), 202

if __name__ == "__main__":
    token = get_token()
    subscribe_to_au_changes(token)
    app.run(port=8000)
AUs vs. Groups: Choosing the Right Tool

Administrative Units and security groups are complementary, not competing. AUs control who can manage identity objects. Groups control what resources identity objects can access. The following scenarios illustrate the correct choice.

Administrative Units solve the tenant-wide delegation problem in Entra ID. Instead of granting User Administrator over all 50,000 users, you can create regional or departmental AUs and assign that role scoped to each AU — giving regional helpdesk teams exactly the access they need without over-privilege. Dynamic AUs extend this with automatic, rule-based membership, and authentication method policy targeting enables controlled rollouts of new authentication methods to pilot populations.
AUs are delegation containers — they do not affect authentication or resource access
Scoped role assignments grant directory roles that apply only to AU members
Dynamic AUs require Entra ID P1; assigned AUs are free
Restricted AUs control who can manage AU membership itself
Only ~10 Entra ID roles support AU scoping — verify before designing your delegation model
Audit AU membership and scoped assignments regularly to prevent privilege drift
